title: {key:z.} / {key:z+} / {key:z-} — Scroll and Land on First Non-Blank
subtitle: Like {key:zz}/{key:zt}/{key:zb}, but the cursor jumps to the first non-blank.
summary: z. centers the line and moves the cursor to the first non-blank. z- puts the line at the bottom and lands at first non-blank. z+ scrolls forward and puts the next-page-top line at the top.

blocks.text: Three more {key:z}-family scroll commands. They behave like {key:z}{key:z} / {key:z}{key:t} / {key:z}{key:b} but also move the cursor to the *first non-blank* of the line — exactly where the code starts.

blocks.label: Like zz, but cursor → first non-blank
Scroll forward, line below current page top → top, cursor first non-blank
Like zb, but cursor → first non-blank

blocks.caption: z+ — page forward to first non-blank

blocks.title: Why Two Sets of z-Scrolls?
blocks.text:
{key:z}{key:z}/{key:z}{key:t}/{key:z}{key:b} preserve the cursor's column. Useful if you want to keep the column for a follow-up motion. They came from the original `vi`.

{key:z}{key:.}/{key:z}{key:+}/{key:z}{key:-} additionally move the cursor to the first non-blank. Almost always what you want when navigating code — landing in the middle of leading whitespace is rarely useful.

Mnemonic: the punctuation versions "clean up" the cursor position; the letter versions don't. Pick whichever you find ergonomic.
{key:z}{key:Enter} is a synonym for {key:z}{key:+}'s effect of "line below current top → new top, cursor first non-blank". The lessons cover {key:z}{key:+}; both keystrokes work.

rows: Cursor → first non-blank of new top
